title: Fetch and HTTP responses

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Fetch and HTTP responses; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Fetch Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Fetch and HTTP responses
sources.claim: fetch resolves for HTTP error statuses and rejects mainly for network-level failures, so code checks response.ok before parsing.

canonicalExplanation.summary: fetch resolves for HTTP error statuses and rejects mainly for network-level failures, so code checks response.ok before parsing.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A request checks response.ok, validates JSON shape, and presents separate HTTP, parse, and network errors. Your goal is to implement and test a JSON request that distinguishes HTTP failure from network rejection. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Call stack and event loop model; Closures and lifetime; async and await; Object records and property access. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: fetch resolves for HTTP error statuses and rejects mainly for network-level failures, so code checks response.ok before parsing. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Bodies are one-use streams, parsing can fail, CORS controls access, and credentials and timeouts need explicit policy. This draft therefore rejects: fetch rejects automatically for every 404 or 500 response.

includes: Construct requests and handle status, headers, body parsing, network failures and cross-origin constraints.
Implement and test a JSON request that distinguishes HTTP failure from network rejection.
excludes: Bodies are one-use streams, parsing can fail, CORS controls access, and credentials and timeouts need explicit policy.

outcomes: Implement and test a JSON request that distinguishes HTTP failure from network rejection.

examples.title: Fetch and HTTP responses: concrete case
examples.situation: A request checks response.ok, validates JSON shape, and presents separate HTTP, parse, and network errors.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A request checks response.ok, validates JSON shape, and presents separate HTTP, parse, and network errors.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: fetch can fulfill with an HTTP error response; check response.ok or status before treating it as success.
examples.result: The result follows because fetch can fulfill with an HTTP error response; check response.ok or status before treating it as success.

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and test a JSON request that distinguishes HTTP failure from network rejection. Keep this boundary in view: Bodies are one-use streams, parsing can fail, CORS controls access, and credentials and timeouts need explicit policy.
